segments.text: Okay,
uh,
commissioner,
um, welcome everyone, um, welcome to the New Zealand Police.
Uh, we've got three hours with you, commissioner, and your team.
It's a long time. Um, we have a structured agenda, so I'm going to
try and keep us within that structure. You'll have it.
Um, and I would ask, as we get questions and different people
t- have to answer them, please just feel free to come up and
speak into the microphone because it makes it so much easier for- for us to
hear, uh, some of the answers. So, on that note, Commissioner,
apparently you've got six minutes.
(laughs)
Right. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Uh, look, uh, can I firstly introduce,
introduce my colleagues beside me?
Andrea Connell, chief operating officer, acting Deputy Commissioner Jill Rogers,
and acting deputy, Deputy Commissioner Tasha Penny.
Thanks for the opportunity today.
Um,
can I just acknowledge, firstly, all of the committee members here with us and the
rest of my team and, and other people here in the room?
Um, the last time I was here at the annual review was five days into my time
as the commissioner of police. Well, it's been a big year, frankly.
Um, it started in the worst possible way with the loss of,
uh, Lyn Fleming on the 1st of January, and it's been pretty tough,
frankly, uh, ever since. But, um, I'm very, very proud of the team that I have
supporting me, uh, my colleagues across the country, uh, and I thank,
uh, not only them for the work they do at the front line, but also, um, the
public of New Zealand and everybody in this room because everybody does
appreciate that policing isn't easy.
And we've had our challenges, not only obviously starting so, so sadly
with the loss of Lyn, but many other things that we've had to navigate through the
year. Uh, and most recently, of course, the IPCA report.
That gives us plenty to think about, uh, moving forward.
So, um, uh, there's a lot to be very, very proud of though,
and as the commissioner of police, I am very, very proud of the results that we
have seen over the last 12 months in terms of policing gangs.
I think about the 500 and how hard we have worked, um, to
achieve that target, uh, as early as we can, but making sure we recruit the right
people into New Zealand Police for our country.
I think about the differences that we have made, uh, in retail
crime, uh, and, and many, many other things.
Uh, government targets, youth violence, um,
uh, the infl- the impact that we've had with our increase in beat
patrols. Um, certainly the expectations that I outlined on day
one as the commissioner, um, have proven to be the right things and they will
remain firmly our priorities moving forward.
Um, we certainly have a lot to, to think about in 2026.
Um, I'm looking very, very forward to, uh, having a new leadership
team, uh, supporting me help steer New Zealand Police and the
colleagues, uh, across the country who do an outstanding job day and
night. Um, amongst, uh, a number of the things that we need to
think about, and I know it's first on the agenda, is of course the audit report,
and I welcome the, uh, areas of focus there, whether that's contract management,
asset management, procurement, um, or the
breach of appropriation that we had in road policing.
Those four things in particular, um, we, uh, are already working
hard to be better at. Um, uh, of course, uh,
given the events of this year, trust and confidence is, is an absolute priority for
me. I said that on day one, nothing has changed. That matters.
We've taken some hits and we will make sure we move forward and, uh, do our
absolute best for our country. Uh, amongst other things for
2026, um, I've been clear, uh, with my executives
that, um, those four priorities I articulated here a year ago
remain. That is focus on core policing, supporting the front
line, uh, leadership, and our role, uh, being
connected to the front line, um, our visibility, and of course, our
accountability under leadership are as important, if not more so
than they were 12 months ago. And of course, our fiscal
responsibility, ensuring that New Zealand Police...
uh, the New Zealand public can have confidence in New Zealand Police in terms of
vote police and our little over $3 billion that we have to deliver services to the
country. Um, I, uh, through the year, I have
also outlined three particular goals, which are fairly simple.
Uh, s- service, safety, and trust.
Those three things matter to all New Zealanders and they matter to us, uh, in
policing as a result of expectations.
I have also, um, started early work on putting in place some of our
own internal targets that, uh, I want out my organization, uh, to
aspire to, which will, uh, drive, uh, behaviors.
Uh, and those targets are 80%, uh, trust and confidence.
Uh, we've been there before. There's no reason we can't do it again.
Um, uh, we have seen some small incremental increases.
We've taken some hits on trust and confidence, um, particularly recently, but
I want to set an expectation that we, um, achieve 80%.
And that will take time, but we'll work really hard towards it.
Another, um, another target that I want to put in place for the organization is
80%, um, satisfaction for the services that we
deliver. We're currently sitting at about 71%, so we've got some work to
do there too, but I know that we can do it.
Uh, and also, uh, 15%, uh, increase in resolutions
for retail crime in particular. We have seen incredible work across the country t-
addressing retail crime. Uh, and I want that to continue.
Um, and then a 15% reduction in public place
violence. So, uh, that's, uh, particularly important in CBDs across New
Zealand. Uh, and that in particular drives behavior for beat patrols
and visibility of police. Many initiatives, uh, um,
underway across the organization to help us be, uh, our absolute best for the
country. Uh, one of the things that I have, um, particularly focused
on more recently is, uh, a thing called, called Frontline Service Model.
And that is that we have over 600 sworn staff who aren't in
necessarily frontline roles, uh, and creating expectations for them to
also get out and make a contribution to policing on the front line.
Increases our presence across the country.
Um-Lots of things to be thinking about.
Uh, I know that we can get there. It's been a tough year, but I, I, I think and,
and I welcome questions from the committee, um, uh, uh, one of the, one of
the final points that I'm, I'm very, very proud of is that if I look at
the, uh, diversity that we have brought into New Zealand Police over the last five
years, in particular, um, Maori communities, our Pacific
communities and ethni- ethnic communities, if I think about Maori communities in
particular over the last five years, we've had a 20% increase in Maori colleagues
in New Zealand Police and I think that's a success story.
And I said in this very place a year ago that that matters, and it's the
responsibility of all members of, uh, New Zealand Police, uh, in
particular the leadership, it's a value that we all subscribe to that we
find better ways to achieve outcomes for all communities across New Zealand.
So thanks, uh, thanks for everyone's time today and I welcome
questions.
Thank you, uh, Commissioner, and I acknowledge the work of the police.
I should say that at the outset. Right.
First question from, uh, Honorable Trinny Anderson.
Thank you very much, uh, thank you Commissioner.
What work is being carried out by police to mitigate the cost pressures and deliver
the 500 new police?
Uh, well, we, um, certainly started a year ago, um, uh,
with a lot of our cost-saving initiatives.
In fact, even, uh, one of the first things I did was a na- a restructure of the
executive to trim down and slim down and actually re-identify opportunities to
reinvest. Um, that, uh, I've been very
clear that with the initiatives that we've rolled out over the last 12 months, that
that doesn't impact on the front line.
Front line is New Zealand Police and my job, along with my executive is
to, is to support them. So we've, uh, we've had a very close look
at the opportunities to not only save but also to reinvest and I'm particularly
proud of where we, uh, have made savings, in particular contractors and
consultants, uh, with a saving of r- um, close to $90 million over the
last, um, uh, a little over a year. Um, those are all
opportunities to reinvest. So we're, we're looking really, really hard, um,
we're... It's one of our priorities, fiscal responsibility.
And, and when will the 500 be delivered?
Um, as soon as possible. I did say, of course, a year ago
that, um, uh, we were working really hard to reach the
500 but that I wasn't prepared to compromise, um, on the, uh,
caliber of New Zealand Police officer.
Um, uh, this year if... to give you a couple of statistics,
we... over the last 12 months, we've had over...
actually close to 9,000 applications.
Uh, the 12 months prior to that was a little over 5,000.
Um, this year we will have, a- as Christmas approaches, nearly, uh,
have, um, signed on another 900 staff.
That's about 800 brand new recruits and about close to 100
rejoins.
Mm-hmm.
Uh, we've never achieved that many in a 12-month period and we've worked incredibly
hard to, um, to promote New Z- uh, policing, uh, in
New Zealand as a career and e- even with some of our colleagues who have gone
across to Australia, we've had some big successes with them coming home.
So working really, really hard, um, I'm hopeful that we'll get
there, uh, as, as early as possible in 2026 but I...
as I've, as I've always said, I, I, I'm not about to compromise on the
standards, uh, of the, uh,
So, um, so from the calculations, the p- police to population
ratio ha- has, has dropped over the course of this government.
There was funding in place for one police officer to every
480 New Zealanders. That's now changed to around one police
officer to every 510 New Zealanders.
Do you, do you have any particular explanation for that increase given your
focus on increased police visibility?
Yeah, look, I think this, this, this week, uh, 10,451
sworn staff, uh, in a couple of weeks' time we'll have another, uh, 80
graduate and few, um, a month after that and, and 91
we've got a target of 500. What I'm focused on is making sure
that we achieve the 500 target but alongside that as I spoke
about is an in- is an initiative to ensure that sworn staff, whatever
their role in New Zealand Police, particularly those that aren't, uh, front line as
a core function, but they also get out and make a contribution.
So I'm drawing on all the levers that I can internally to ensure that we, uh,
we do our best and, and visibility does matter and over 600
staff that are sworn but non-front line, uh, all have a contribution
to make just like I do and I'll be out there as a policeman doing my bit
Do you intend to restore that one to 480 ratio that was
previously funded?
Uh, that, well, that's, that's not so much a decision for me.
W- what is for me is to ensure that I, um, uh,
we, we work really, really hard to recruit that 500 and ma- and
optimize the, the total actual strength of New Zealand Police officers.
That's what I've got to focus on because-
... w- we can have-
Just one last thing.
... we can have statistics about, you know, ratios and things like that but I, I've
got to take what's in front of me and that is 500 new staff and making sure that my
existing staff are all making a contribution.
The, um, the annual report indicates there's been a decline in general
public who agree that police are effectively responding to
crime. Uh, do you think that has a correlation to the
drop in the police to population ratio?
Sorry, say that again, sorry?
So in your annual report, there's been a decline when people are asked, the general
public, "Do you agree that police are effectively responding to serious
crime?"
That's dropped in your annual report over the past couple of years.
Right.
Do you think there's a link between that decrease and the fact that
there are fewer police per other peop- per population
in New
Zealand? Yeah, well it's i- it's interesting, a lot of the feedback that I've had throughout
the year given our emphasis on beat patrols and visibility is that in fact
pretty much across all communities the feedback I'm getting is that we see a lot
more police out there, uh, whether that's on the roads or on the beat.
So
th- uh, that's my experience, uh, and certainly that's the experience that I'm
drawing from members of the public that tell me, putting aside
ratios. In terms of our responsiveness, uh, I mean, that is
directly related to the priorities that I've set about being a responsive police
service, um, service being one of our goals.
So we will
w- we will work incredibly hard to provide the best possible service, uh, ac-
across all the demands that police has to manage.
Okay. We're gonna take two more sups and then we'll move on to a new question.
Uh, Mr. Bates.
Thanks, Chair. Just going back, Commissioner, to your comment regarding the number
of rejoins from Australia.
Mm-hmm.
What is driving those officers coming home?
And what more are you doing to bring more of those-
Yeah. ... officers back to New Zealand?
Yeah. Thank you, good question. I, um, have spoken to many of
those who either have returned to New Zealand or intend to return to
New Zealand, and, uh, in a, in a way, it actually happened by accident when I was
over there and I spoke to some that had, had decided to go to
Australia. Um, the common theme from
pretty much all of them, and as most recently as Sunday night when I spoke to one
who is currently with Queensland Police but wants to return, um, in
the next few months-
Right.
... is that, yes, while financially it may be a different story to tell,
um, when, when you go and experience policing in a different
place, you see, uh, what it can be like,
um, when you think about the tools that some do or don't have,
as the case may be in Australia, to help them police, uh, the
camaraderie, um, the emphasis that's placed on
working with all communities, in particular for our country, the pride that we
have in working with Maori. Um,
there's a whole host of reasons, and those are just a selection
of some of the, uh, responses that I do get.
But it's fairly consistent when I ask the why, as to why they wanna come
home. And having, uh, been offshore myself, and I
just returned on Sunday night from, uh, Morocco where I attended
the Interpol's General Assembly, uh, the biggest event for police
chiefs around the world, um, oh, I'm constantly reminded
of how lucky we are to live in New Zealand, to be Kiwis, and for me as
a policeman, uh, alongside 15,000 colleagues who do a, an
incredibly, um, good job. Actually, when you see it outside your own
country, you realize how lucky we are.
So those are the common themes that I hear wherever I go and whoever I speak
to.
Great. And, uh, just a second sup regarding the
measures that the member was just referring to that were tracking down
for those two references for people's feeling of crime-
(clears throat)
... coming down to 71, 73
when, when the government became the government, stabilizing.
What's your forecast for those, um, indicators over the
coming year?
In terms of our response?
In terms of the percentage of people who agree or strongly agree that police that
deal effectively with serious crime-
Yeah.
... and the second one's most harmful, both of those tracking down from the
high 70s-
Yeah.
Yeah.
... when we became the government.
Yes. Yeah, well-
That in mind, what's your forecast?
So, forec- hard to predict, uh, what the future might looks like-
Yeah.
... other than to be able to talk to the priorities that I've set, the importance
of trust and confidence, our visibility in communities across the country, and
how hard we are working to be as present as possible and, and
respond to all the different demands on New Zealand Police.
So, uh, our intent is the right intent, and we hope that those,
um, statistics, um, get better.
Right. I'm going to Miss Paul. Uh, just remind members, audit,
we're talking about the audit here. Yep.
Sorry, Mr. Chair.
We're working through the structure-
Yep.
... moving this forward.
Yeah.
Um, kia ora, uh, Commissioner. My question is around,
um,
uh, your executive restructure and how that's going.
So I understand that that's a process that's happening at the moment, or-
Uh-
... where, where are we at with that?
Yes, look, I did a restructure, uh, very, uh, early on in my tenure where we
trimmed it down. I felt that it was too big, too bloated, too expensive, and not
delivering for the organization or New Zealand, so I trimmed down.
Um, and at the moment, uh, we are going through
processes to appoint, uh, new deputy commissioners
that will inevitably result in appointments at assistant commissioner level
and also district commander level.
So really in the top, um, three layers
of New Zealand Police, we are going through processes to put new leadership
in place, and I'm really looking forward to that-
Mm-hmm.
... and starting 2026 with a new team and
substantive people in, in positions.
Uh, I've managed through the year without that.
So basically about simplifying the executive
structure, is that-
Making it, certainly making it more lean, but not changing any of our
focus, uh, the things that we value. None of that is changing.
It's simply saying that where there are savings potentially to be
made, what do those look like, how can we achieve them, how can we reinvest
them?
(clears throat)
100% (whispers) . So, um, priorities don't change with, with a change in
structure. Um, uh, roles and responsibilities
change because then inevitably what it means is senior leaders pick up more because
there's less to pick up often the same workload, but we're up for
that. Um, our, our value set doesn't change.
Um, um, but, uh, having people in positions and
confirmed matters, and I'm, I'm looking forward to that.
Yeah, that's really helpful because obviously the executive structure is gonna
dictate how your operations work.
Um, I have, um, an OIA around the executive
restructure that, um, says that you're considering
disestablishing the deputy chief executive iwi and communities role
within the restructure. Is that something you're considering?
No, that, that happened a year ago. Yeah.
And why did you do that?
Uh, that was about trimming down the executive level.
As I've s- as I've said, it doesn't change the value set that we have.
In fact, um, if I think about that particular role, it places greater emphasis
on, uh, who is in the executive to, to deliver on expectations.
And, and, and one of our, um, core values is commitment to Maori and the treaty.
Um, we wear it on our shoulders every day. Um, I'm very passionate about that.
In fact, it's one of the things that, in my travels and work offshore, I think New
Zealand can be proud of.
(clears throat)
Doesn't, doesn't change the emphasis that we place on that and I...
And as I said in this room a year ago-...
um, you know, the work that we do with all communities across New Zealand,
particularly at a grassroots level, our, uh, iwi liaison
offices, our Maori responsiveness managers,
uh, across all our structures, both operationally and corporate, we all
subscribe to the same values. So, in the restructure that I did earlier
this year, uh, that position did g- get taken out along
with, um, a number of others at tier three as well.
Um, doesn't change the emphasis and expectation we put on each other to,
um, to step up.
So, that's picked back up through the iwi liaison offices, or is there a senior-
... person in your structure that is in charge of liaising with communities and
with iwi?
Well, we all are. I mean, whilst we have different roles and responsibilities, a
huge amount of my time as commissioner is spent engaging with
communities. I'm very lucky to have focus forums that provide good advice and
support to me.
Okay, um-
Uh, sorry.
I'm going to go to Mr. Ferris now. We're just talking about audit results, Mr.
Ferris. Um, your question, please.
Yeah, kia ora. Kia ora, Commissioner.
Um, hey, you've noted yourself and the OAG report noted that
trust and confidence is pretty much at an all-time low in the police.
It's down to 69%, hasn't been there since 2020.
Um, now, do you have, or what, what's the equivalent trust level amongst the
Maori, Maori community specifically?
Yeah, look, traditionally, um, trust and confidence across Maori communities
has been lower than that, and it's an area for us to, to work
on. Uh, 60%, in the 60s is the, is the figure lower than the ones
that you just, um, mentioned. Uh, that gives us,
uh, uh, lots to think about. Um, I'm very conscious of
that. But, you know, I do also know and acknowledge that many of the initiatives
that we've put in place across the organization, Te Paerenga being one of
those, are delivering, uh, results for Maori communities that we
can all actually be very, very proud of.
And, um, when, when I think about how we, how police
agencies around the world work with communities, I think New Zealand leads the way
globally.
Yeah, kia ora. Hey, look, six months ago at the last scrutiny, uh, discussion you
and I had, and the minister, you both dismissed the relevance of the
understanding police delivery report for Maori.
Um, given, you know, where we are currently and the recent revelations around the
conduct and attitude of the most senior police officers in the country,
um, and the connection to what Maori experience at the hands of
the police, do you think rejecting that report was a mistake?
Uh, it's not, it's not that we rejected the report.
What, what I did say was that-
Oh, I'm pretty, I'm pretty clear that you did reject the port, the report, both of
you-
Yeah.
... because it was the last executive's report, not your own.
No, look, what I did say that there are certainly some learnings in there for us,
and that's why the executive has gone through all 51 recommendations.
We've adopted 15 of them, we've put 29 of them, uh, on hold
while we, um, prioritize, uh, and we have rejected seven of them.
So, we've gone through-
Okay, but just given, given that low, um, given the low trust and confidence in the
Maori communities, what are your targets to improve that through the, uh, through
So, of the 15 we've adopted, and this will also apply to many of the 29,
we, we are blending that into Te Hiranga o te Tai, which you'll be
familiar with, and the three pou that matter there.
One of those is cultural competency, one of them is around partnerships, and the
other is about initiatives and making sure that we work together with
Maori to deliver better outcomes.
And I, and I know that as an organization, we can be very proud of how hard we've
worked in this space. And as I say, it's not just about bosses, uh,
particularly here in Wellington, it's about all staff who subscribe to the same
value set and, and, and the staff that we have at an operational
level, um, in Maori Pacific and Ethnic Services.
Collectively, we actually are, are working very, very hard and that isn't gonna
change one bit.
Okay, thank you for-
Hey, just last, just lastly,
Mr. Ferris. Mr. Ferris.
... the OAG, the OAG report-
Mr. Ferris.
Yeah, this last, last sup on this one.
Um, we've... This is the last sup, but I'm gonna remind you that we have a section
on Maori, uh, partnerships later at, on, and I just wanna
make sure we could k- keep this on the agenda. So last sup, please.
Yeah, yep, yep. Yep, yep, thanks for that.
Um, okay, so the OAG also found that there was weak outcome reporting
for Maori initiatives. Uh, h- how are you gonna bolster the
activity by having clear targets for police?
(clears throat)
Uh, clear targets for police?
Or specifically to Maori?
Yeah, the, the OAG report found weak outcome reporting around Maori
achievement or advancement.
Right,
So, weak outcome reporting for Maori. So, how are you gonna fix that?
I, I think we need to celebrate successes better and that's certainly something
that in the new year we need to look at, because the results are there.
Uh, if I think about the high percentage of non-reoffending rate in
Te, uh, Te Paerenga as one example, the fact that we've got 12
Rangatahi, uh, TPOs, uh, opportunities across the
country. We've got 30 for, for adults.
It's all there, perhaps we just don't celebrate it enough.
Because if I, uh, think about the progress that we've made in, as an organization
over 30 years in particular, we can't dismiss how much hard work's been put into
this, and that won't be changing under my leadership.
Right. Now, we're gonna move on to organizational health, and the first one's
strategic direction. I'm gonna come back to you, so hopefully you can weave your
question into this. We're gonna start with Honorable Ginny Anderson.
Thank you, Andrew. Uh, Commissioner, do you think that there is a problem with
police culture?
No, I don't. Um, what we have seen in the last 12 months is some,
some events, uh, particularly recently with the IPCA
report, which relates to, uh, conduct of, uh, largely
former members of the most, at the most senior level.
And we've had some other, um, experiences that we've identified,
um, through either own, our own audits and checking processes, whether
that's breath testing devices or it's access to inappropriate
material. Though, those two, two, two last, um, situations in
particular are as consequence of the decisions that
me and my executive have made to, to have a closer look.
Um, that, uh, is very disappointing.
Um, that doesn't mean that the entire organization should be branded in
a way that's unfair, because it's not....
uh, what anyone, uh, deserves. Th- those that do, uh,
fall below expectations, um, my expectation is that we act
on that and we, we deal with them. Um, and I'm confident that
albeit hugely disappointing, um, uh, it's a
reminder to all staff that, uh, expectations are there and
we'll act on anything that falls below standards.
The IPCA have been very clear that they do believe there is a problem
with police culture. Are you taking the position that the IPCA is
incorrect in that assessment?
No. I've taken on board already all the recommendations.
But I think what, what I'm saying is that would be h- would be grossly unfair
to, to reflect on 15,000 men and women who do a tough job and a very
good job across the country day and night. That, that's what I'm saying.
It's, it's, it ... but there, if there are things, uh, that have been
identified, and the IPCA report certainly did within that, the cohort of
people that were subject to part, to that investigation certainly identified an
issue. Um, and I've taken all of those recommendations on board.
My team, we've put together a plan that, um, we're prioritizing in
terms of effort. We've got many things underway and we've got additional things
underway, such as the performance improvement review that I, I'm working with
Public Service Commission on to identify other areas of opportunity, whether that's
integrity or culture or conduct. So, we, we are certainly working very, very
hard to, um, to correct behaviors that fall short of
expectations, not just for me as the Commissioner of Police, but for all New
Zealanders, frankly.
So just to be clear, the, the IPCA specifically said that after the Basley
report there were some, um, improvements made but there was still
much more to be done-
Yeah.
... for police culture in general.
Yeah.
Are you saying that you don't think-
No.
... that that applies?
Yeah. I wouldn't be accepting all the recommendations if I didn't agree with them,
so there are certainly pockets of opportunity and that's why, that's why we're
doing what we're doing, whether it's, uh, the auditing processes to identify those
pockets and so that gives us the opportunity to act on.
But the vast majority of police, um, uh, turn up
do
a, a, a fantastic job and it's, uh, it's expected that we would deal with anything
that falls short and that's, that's exactly what we're doing.
Did you consider having one of your new deputies as non-constabulary as
there was post the Basley inquiry? Uh, I think the precedent was Lyn Provo.
Yeah. That's ... We are, we're working through some options.
That's the sorts of things. I mean that, that, that's, um ...
You know, there's all sorts of things on the table but, you know, I'm looking
forward to having a new leadership team in place.
There are very, very capable, um, experienced and high
caliber, uh, colleagues that are ready to step up.
We've just gotta let processes take their course.
What changes have you made, uh, to police or how have you
changed police culture, uh, during the
2024/'25 financial year?
Uh, well, set expectations from the outset and I've, I've
done quite a bit of that this year, um, whether that's, uh, involving the police
college or it's involving things like retail crime or it's, um,
outlining those four priorities that I've spoken about, if it's the
goals, service, safety, trust. Like there's many different artifacts
or conversations that I can point to, many of which I have led,
uh, that have reinforced what I expect, uh, of my organization and the people in
it.
Sorry. Last one, sorry. And the process to appoint the new deputy commissioners,
is that different to ones that have been run in the past or the same process?
No. Look, I th- well, I mean, I ... The current process that's underway is not
led by me. That's led by the Public Service Commission.
Um, but the work that's going in to ensure that all the right
probity, integrity and all those other checks that New Zealanders would expect
when you're appointing, um, people at the most senior level of the organization,
that is very, uh, intense, uh, and it needs to be.
So that's the difference.

And you can actually see
across most sectors now, most agencies, that, um, service to the public's
improving and, and results are improving.
Trea- Especially, especially with police.
Treasury found that funding had been taken out of core
functions such as asset management and corporate services
and redirected to the frontline, to the point where those fundamental parts
of police were not operating properly.
Do you have a view to want to rectify that or you're happy for that to continue?
Well, they're outstanding. I mean, uh, yes, there's been,
They're outstanding? Corporate services are outstanding, are you saying?
Absolutely. Uh, the, the... Look, in my view as minister, um, the
performance, you talk about property services, they've got a very good plan.
You gotta remember-
You read the report.
You gotta, you gotta remember that when I became minister, I don't l- I know you
don't like hearing this. Um, the police were about a week away from going
broke, breaching their appropriation. And do you know why?
Still don't.
Because their core services had not been funded properly.
We immediately put $120 million into
support, into actually making sure that those core services were
funded. That was meant to be a one-off.
That $120 million is there to stay.
Okay, yeah. I've got a question here from Mr. Bates.
Thanks, Chair. Minister, I just want to go back to the
1:480 ratio.
Can you just help me understand? Was that fully funded by the
previous government on an ongoing basis or was that funded
just in the 2017 budget and then
left?
We're... So, poli- police were going broke when I come, when I took over as
minister, I was briefed-
When?
One of my first briefings was, um, we, uh, th- uh, "In about a week, we'll breach
our appro- appropriation." Which means they were going broke.
Yeah,
The way they were saving money is they weren't recruiting.
Um, because, you know, 70% of, um, police's expenses are,
um, are personnel. Um, so that was
the situation that we were presented with. So we had to stabilize immediately.
Um, and obviously get the recruitment program, um, back up and running at the
college. We extended out to, um, 20 weeks.
And, um, and like I said, I'm confident that we'll deliver those, um, 500
additional police officers this term.
Right. Now, going to a new line of questioning, I think from Ms. Paul.
Yeah. On, uh, road safety. Um, so you've
reiterated over and over again standards and integrity, and we've got
30,000 alcohol breath tests that have been-
Yeah.
... falsely or erroneously recorded.
Yeah.
I'm keen t- for you to share your thoughts on that-
Yeah.
... and what you're doing to remedy the situation.
But I'm also keen to understand with the pilot rolling out for roadside drug
testing in Wellington-
Yeah.
... later this month, what needs to happen in the pilot
for you to, um, deem it a success or a
failure?
Yeah. Okay. So the first one is, um, really disappointing,
obviously. Uh, but that was discovered
because, um, the police are auditing themselves and going through and
actually checking, making sure they're doing health checks exactly in terms
of, um, what has been delivered and how our frontline police officer
del- delivered it. So it was a good thing it was picked up.
Now it's a, uh, employment process that, um, that the police will work through
on that. In terms of, um, roadside, um, drug
testing, yes, the pilot, uh, kicks off in December here in
Wellington. It'll be expanded nationally, should be by the middle of
next year, June, July, uh, next year.
And quite simply the reason for it is, is because I think 30%
of, um, of road fatalities, uh, involve
drugs.
Mm.
Um, so it's obviously gonna make our roads
safer. I don't think, um, Kiwis want to share the roads with people that are under
the influence of THC or, or methamphetamine or,
or, um, MDMA or cocaine.
Yeah. And I fully agree with you on that-
Yeah.
Nobody should ever drive impaired.
I guess the, the, the thing I'm wondering about is an alcohol breath test is
far simpler than a-
Yeah, it is.
... saliva-based testing-
Yeah. ... for four different drugs.
Yeah.
So how... Yeah, what training are you gonna put in place?
I know every police officer now has to undergo breath testing training again.
Yeah.
But how are you gonna make sure this more complex test...
And if your pilot comes back and shows a high number of incorrect
tests, will that make you go back to the drawing board?
Yeah, I think if that... I'll, I'll hand over to the commissioner 'cause that's
largely operational, the question that you've asked.
But, um-
He has given me his answer earlier though,
Oh, yeah. It's okay,
yeah. ... happy to just take yours
(laughs)
Yeah, well (laughs) go ahead.
Unless he wants to add anything new, but-
It is, it is... It's just that three-quarters of that question's operational.
I just, from the, from the testing side of it, the pilot, obviously the feedback,
if we get the feedback and there's issues around that, then we're just gonna have
to look at that and make sure that we've... yeah.
We make the changes.
That's helpful. Thank you.
Yeah.
All right. We're coming near to the end.
So any more questions?
Always got one. (laughs)
All right. Couple more from you.
Um, Minister, there's been an increase in gang membership of about
800 over the past, I think it's just over 18
months. Um, are you concerned that the number of gang members
might be higher than the number of police officers in the near future?
I'm, I'm not concerned at all about the way our police are,
um, uh, policing gangs. I think they're just, quite
simply they're dominating the gangs.
Um, being a gang member in New Zealand now is a pretty miserable
occupation. I think that, um, yes, the police will continue to use the
gang list as a tool, um, to be, to be able to
monitor. Um, the number will go up and down a bit.
I noticed that, um, that without a doubt the number of people going on
to the, that gang register has reduced significantly.
Um-
So, no, the, there's 800 more, you know that don't
Yeah, but it's, but under the, I'd have to go back and check the numbers if it was
under the pre, your lot it would probably be more like 2,000.
It was going up, um-
I think it's 8,000.
She's making that up aren't you?
Well go, well go back and check it.
Free styling it.
I don't have the numbers but-
No, you clearly don't.
... but I, but I know-
Don't need them.
... but I can tell you this for sure,
the number of people going on to the gang register has reduced, has slowed
down. Um, without a doubt. I'm sorry guys, but you're just gonna have
to face the reality that the gangs under the previous government,
um, got away with blue murder. They, they took over the
streets.
There was a, so-
They intimidated people. The violence, it was, you know,
for us, for New Zealand it was totally unacceptable.
So, over a 12-month period-
You
guys took your eyes off the ball big time. ... we saw a 97% increase in methamphetamine
manufacture-
Yep, so we got a meth
plan, we got a meth plan on that. ... and consumption in New Zealand and we've also heard, um, from, from
your own senior detectives that gangs are making
double the profits from methamphetamine during the time of that gang
patch ban. Do, do you see that there would be a perception that it
would be largely superficial if people don't see them but they're still just making
record profits behind the scenes?
Well, methamphetamine is insidious as a drug,
um, I remember clearly when we first started seeing meth on the
streets. Um, yes, it's for organized crime,
transnational organized crime. It's a big source of revenue, um,
and that is why we've just announced our, our meth plan to start to
deal with it and really start to, um, hook into it.
It's, it's, the increase hasn't showed up.
We've been waiting for it to show up.
It hasn't really showed up in the health system.
Um, it hasn't really showed up with policing, in terms of a big
increase in meth-related, um, violence or, or crime.
Um, so, you know, it's only a, um, it's only a theory but the
theory is that, um, that those that are using meth are using more of
it, uh, because of course it's got to pass through the body to be detected in the,
um, in the wastewater testing. But the fact of the matter is we've got a meth
problem in New Zealand, there's no doubt about that.
We have for a long time, it's been increasing for a long time and we're really
committed to making sure that we do the work that we need to do to get, to start
shutting that down.
Do you think the gang patch ban has had any material difference on the
amount of money and assets and profits
that organized criminal groups are able to, uh,
accumulate in New Zealand?
I think the gang patch ban has been extremely effective for
the police to, um, to start to have another tool to deal
with gangs. And like I said to you, the gangs are
disproportionately responsible for the level of, uh, violent
offending in this country. We all know that they're the ones that are
running, um, the transnational crime and the, and the drugs, as you've just
highlighted. Has the gang patch ban and the gang legislation that we
brought in been effective? All the feedback that I get from the front
line
and, uh, that, so the people that are actually using it, the people that are out
there dealing with the gangs, is that it's been highly effective.
Right, I am going to give the last question to Miss
Knightley.
Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, following on from that, speaking of tools,
um,
I too am hearing from a lot of the police, um, excellent police people
that the gang patch, um, law is helping them a lot.
So, with the recent figures that my colleague mentioned earlier, um, that we've
smashed the target that we had with respect to reducing the number
of victims of violent crime, um, re- reduced now
by 38,000, would you say Minister and Commissioner that
passing laws like these, giving police the extra
tools to deal with, um, disgusting behavior
helps that ultimate goal of reducing victims, the number of
victims?
Yeah, I do. I just want to say there, there's two sides to this.
One, with policing you kind of got, got to go right at the problem and you've got
to deal with it and that's, that's what the police are doing.
Um, the other side of it that's not always vili- visible but they're actually
highly effective is identifying the families that need support and
help. Um, because you want to break that, especially that
intergenerational, the family sort of, um, situation where the
kids are growing, are born into an environment where they're probably on a fast
track into the youth justice system and, and then into the adult, um,
justice system. So, um, yes, without a doubt it's been very effective
in, in reducing the amount of victimizations. We're gonna keep doing that.
Um, but the other piece of work that's got to happen alongside of that is,
um, is the social side of it. Getting in and helping these families and, uh, making
sure they get the support they need.
And yes, likewise from me. I constantly hear from staff across the
country that the new legislation has empowered them to do their
jobs. Uh, they love it, uh, and it's translating
into results that communities value and that is, uh, feelings of
safety. And that's a positive.
Thank you.
Right, um, just I'd like to say on behalf of the
Justice Select Committee, thank you to all the police and, uh,
particularly you Commissioner for three hours of intense questioning.
I, I just want to recognize all the senior leaders in the
police. You operate in a very difficult environment.
You're doing a great thing for New Zealand and I want to thank you Commissioner
and your team. I also just want to acknowledge the Minister for
volunteering to turn up this afternoon and answering the question,
questions in an open manner. I thank you also.
So thank you everyone, it's been a very helpful session
and I think New Zealand's better for it.
Thank you, Mr. Chair.
Yeah, thank
you. Thank you.
See you later.
(crosstalk)
